elements.sent-2411-001: We define linear and radial stretch maps in the affine-additive group, and prove that they are minimizers of the mean quasiconformal distortion functional.
elements.sent-2411-002: For the proofs we use a method based on the notion of modulus of a curve family and the minimal stretching property (MSP) of the afore-mentioned maps.
elements.sent-2411-003: MSP relies on certain given curve families compatible with the respective geometric settings of the strech maps.
elements.section-2411-001: Introduction and statement of the main results
elements.sent-2411-004: The Gr\"otzsch problem can be formulated as follows: let $a>1$, consider a square $Q=(0,1)\times(0,1)$ and a rectangle $R=(0,a)\times(0,1)$.
elements.sent-2411-005: We ask if there is a conformal map which maps the horizontal edges of $Q$ into the corresponding horizontal edges of $R_a$, and requiring respectively the same condition on the vertical edges.
elements.sent-2411-006: It turns out that there is no such conformal mapping; however, using complex notation one finds that the linear stretch map given by \begin{equation}x+iy\mapsto ax+iy,\end{equation} solves the Gr\"otzsch problem and it is the closest to be conformal.
elements.sent-2411-007: The works of Gr\"otzsch \cite{G , \cite{AP , established criteria to measure how to approximate conformality.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Gr\"otzsch also formulated the analogous problem between annuli in the complex plane and proved that the solution is the radial stretch map which, for $k>0$, is given by \begin{equation}z\mapsto|z|^{k-1}z.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-009: Via the formal substitution $z=e^{\xi+i\psi}$ one sees that the radial stretch can be seen as the linear map given by $(\xi,\psi)\mapsto(k\xi,\psi)$.
elements.sent-2411-010: It is also worth mentioning that Astala \cite{Ast , used the radial stretch map to prove the sharpness on the optimal Sobolev exponent for $K$-quasiconformal mappings in the complex plane.}
elements.sent-2411-011: An extremal quasiconformal map is a minimizer for the maximal distortion among some class of quasiconformal mappings.
elements.sent-2411-012: Methods involving the modulus of curve families were used to identify such extremality between annuli in the complex plane by Balogh, F\"assler and Platis \cite{BFP-C .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Motivated by Mostow's rigidity \cite{M-R , the Heisenberg groups became an interesting setting for studying quasiconformal maps.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The related theory reached an advanced study thanks to Pansu \cite{Pansu , and Kor\'anyi-Reimann \cite{KR1 , \cite{KR2 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: We underline that as in the Euclidean case (see Gehring and V\"ais\"al\"a \cite{GV ), quasiconformal maps of the Heisenberg group can distort the Hausdorff dimension with arbitrary fashion, see Balogh \cite{B .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Balogh, F\"assler and Platis constructed appropriate analogues of linear and radial stretch maps for the first Heisenberg group, \cite{BFP-H-MM .}
elements.sent-2411-017: For the latter case the same authors subsequently proved that the radial stretch map is an essentially unique minimizer for the mean distortion functional, \cite{BFP-H-U .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper we search for extremal quasiconformal maps on a different metric measure space: that is, the affine-additive group, which from the topological viewpoint is one of the eight $3$-dimensional Thurston geometries, see \cite{Thu .}
elements.sent-2411-019: In \cite{BBP1 the authors prove that the affine-additive group is conformally hyperbolic.}
elements.sent-2411-020: This is in contrast with the Heisenberg group which is conformally parabolic, see Zorich in \cite{Z .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In particular, the two spaces are not quasiconformally equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-022: The main obstacle given by this inequivalence is that the maps which are quasiconformal in the first Heisenberg group are not compatible with the quasiconfomal maps of the affine-additive group.
elements.sent-2411-023: Therefore becomes interesting to study the quasiconformal maps on the affine-additive group as well as to seek extremal maps for a mean distortion functional adapted to the metric and the measure structures of the considered space.
elements.sent-2411-024: We implement a suitable version of the modulus method which is evidently useful for our purposes.
elements.sent-2411-025: As a consequence we are able to present new examples of self-mappings of the affine-additive group which share some remarkable features.
elements.sent-2411-026: Following the work of Balogh, F\"assler and Platis \cite{BFP-H-MM , we define a mapping having the "minimal stretching property" (MSP) for a given curve family by adapting it to our particular case in the sub-Riemannian framework.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In the first part we build a modulus method which relies on the minimal stretching property of the map for a given curve family foliating the domain of definition.
elements.sent-2411-028: In the second and third parts we show that this designed method detects quasiconformal maps between domains of the affine-additive group.
elements.sent-2411-029: The effect is that we obtain extremal stretch maps minimizing the mean distortion functional in the class of all quasiconformal mappings between two such domains.
elements.sent-2411-030: We begin by describing the affine-additive group; in particular, its sub-Riemannian structure, and a preliminary metric notion of quasiconformal mappings for this group.
elements.sent-2411-031: For more details about the affine-additive group, we refer to \cite{Bubani .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Our starting point is the hyperbolic plane, defined as \begin{equation}\bH_\C^1:=\{x+iy\in\C:x>0,\,y\in\R\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-033: For fixed $\lambda>0,\,t\in\R$, we consider affine transformations $f_{\lambda,t :\bH_\C^1\to\bH_\C^1$, defined by \begin{equation}f_{\lambda,t}(x+iy)=\lambda(x+iy)+it.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-034: We observe that $\bH_\C^1$ is in bijection with the set of transformations of the above form: to each point $\lambda+it$ we uniquely assign the transformation $f_{\lambda,t}$.
elements.sent-2411-035: Therefore we define a group structure on $\bH_\C^1$ by considering the composition of any two transformations $f_{\lambda',t'}$ and $f_{\lambda,t}$: \begin{eqnarray}(f_{\lambda',t'}\circ f_{\lambda,t})(x+iy) = f_{\lambda'\lambda,\;\lambda't+t'}(x+iy).\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-036: Based on this we introduce the group operation on $\bH_\C^1$ by \begin{equation}(\lambda'+it')\star_0(\lambda+it)=\lambda'(\lambda+it)+it'.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-037: This operation is extended over the space $ \R\times\bH_\C^1 $ as follows: we take the Cartesian product of the additive group $(\R,+)$ and the group $(\bH_\C^1,\star_0)$.
elements.sent-2411-038: Then, if $p'=(a',\lambda'+it')$ and $p=(a,\lambda+it)$ belong to $\R\times\bH_\C^1$, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq-mult} p'\star p=(a'+a,\lambda'(\lambda+it)+it')\in\R\times\bH_\C^1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-039: The pair $\Aa=(\R\times\bH_\C^1,\;\star)$ shall be called the affine-additive group.
elements.sent-2411-040: We define the following $1$-form on $\Aa$: \begin{equation}\label{contact form omega} \vartheta=\frac{dt}{2\lambda}-da.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-041: Then the pair $(\Aa,\vartheta)$ is a contact manifold.
elements.sent-2411-042: A basis for the Lie algebra comprises the following left invariant vector fields: \begin{equation}U =\partial_a+2\lambda\partial_t, \ V =2\lambda\partial_\lambda, \ W =-\partial_a.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-043: The horizontal tangent bundle of $\Aa$ is \begin{equation}H_{\Aa}=\ker\vartheta=Span\{U,V\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-044: We denote by $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\Aa}$ the sub-Riemannian metric on $\mathcal{H _{\Aa}$ which makes $\{ U, V\}$ an orthonormal basis.}
elements.sent-2411-045: An absolutely continuous curve $\gamma:[c,d]\rightarrow\Aa$ is called horizontal if and only if $\dot\gamma(s)\in\ker\vartheta_{\gamma(s)}$ for almost every $s\in[c,d]$ and its horizontal velocity is given by $|\dot\gamma|_H=\sqrt{\langle\dot\gamma,\dot\gamma\rangle_\Aa}$.
elements.sent-2411-046: The associated sub-Riemannian distance is denoted by $d_\Aa$.
elements.sent-2411-047: The left-invariant Haar measure for $\Aa$ is $ d\mu_\Aa=\frac{1 {\lambda^2}\,da\, d\lambda\, dt.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The authors proved in \cite{BBP1 that the conformal type of the affine-additive group is hyperbolic.}
elements.sent-2411-049: A metric definition of quasiconformality (in the sense of Heinonen and Koskela \cite{HK ) is given in terms of the sub-Riemannian distance on the affine-additive group as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-050: If $\Omega,\Omega'$ are two domains in $\Aa$, a homeomorphism $f: \Omega\to \Omega'$ is called quasiconformal if there exists $1\le H<\infty$ such that \begin{equation}\label{metric qc} \limsup_{r\rightarrow0}\frac{\sup_{d_\Aa(p,q)\leq r}d_\Aa(f(p),f(q))}{\inf_{d_\Aa(p,q)\geq r}d_\Aa(f(p),f(q))}=:H_f(p)\leq H,\quad \text{for all }p \in \Aa.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}\label{metric qc} \limsup_{r\rightarrow0}\frac{\sup_{d_\Aa(p,q)\leq r}d_\Aa(f(p),f(q))}{\inf_{d_\Aa(p,q)\geq r}d_\Aa(f(p),f(q))}=:H_f(p)\leq H,\quad for all p \in \Aa.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-051: There also exist an analytic as well as a geometric definition for quasiconformal mappings of $\Aa$.
elements.sent-2411-052: It turns out that both are equivalent to the above metric definition.
elements.sent-2411-053: This fact was already well-known for quasiconformal maps of $\C$ and also known for general sub-Riemannian manifolds; we provide an Appendix which illustrates the details for the case of $\Aa$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Such quasiconformal mappings share Sobolev regularity properties and also satisfy the contact condition, meaning that they preserve the contact form $\vartheta$, i.e. $f^*\vartheta=\sigma\vartheta$ almost everywhere for some non-vanishing smooth function $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-055: This imposes some rigidity on the quasiconformal mappings of smooth type; on the other hand, the contact condition is a quite straightforward requirement for a candidate map.

elements.sent-2411-360: Any curve $\gamma$ in $\Aa$ shall be always considered continuous.
elements.sent-2411-361: The points on a curve $\gamma : [c, d] \to \Aa$ are denoted by \begin{equation}\gamma(s)=(\gamma_1(s),\gamma_I(s))\in\R\timesH^1_\C.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-362: An absolutely continuous curve $\gamma:[c,d]\to\Aa$ (in the Euclidean sense) is called horizontal if \begin{equation}\dot\gamma(s)\in\ker\vartheta_{\gamma(s)}\quad for almost every  s\in[c,d].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-363: The length of a horizontal curve corresponds to \begin{equation}\ell(\gamma)=\int_c^d|\dot\gamma(s)|_H\,ds.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-364: We say that $\gamma$ is rectifiable when $\ell(\gamma)$ is finite, moreover we say that $\gamma$ is locally rectifiable when all its closed sub-curves are rectifiable.
elements.sent-2411-365: If $\gamma:[c,d]\to\Aa$ is a rectifiable curve, the line integral over $\gamma$ of a Borel function $\rho:\Aa\to[0,\infty]$ is defined as \begin{equation}\int_\gamma \rho \,d\ell=\int_c^d \rho(\gamma(s))|\dot\gamma(s)|_H\,ds,\end{equation} and in case $\gamma$ is only locally rectifiable, we set \begin{equation}\int_\gamma \rho \,d\ell=\sup\left\{\int_{\gamma'} \rho\,d\ell:\gamma' is a rectifiable subcurve of \gamma\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-366: The definition for the conformally invariant $4$-modulus of a family $\Gamma$ of curves in $\Aa$ has been given in the introduction, see \eqref{def 4-Mod .}
elements.sent-2411-367: For curves $\gamma : (a, b) \to \Aa$ we shall employ the notion of local rectifiability.
elements.sent-2411-368: It is worth to say that a family which consists only of curves that are not locally rectifiable has modulus zero, \cite{HKST-book .}
elements.sent-2411-369: All quasiconformal mappings of the affine-additive group are absolutely continuous on almost every curve, \cite{HKST-paper , \cite{Shan .}}
elements.sent-2411-370: To sum up, given a quasiconformal map $f : \Omega \to \Omega'$ between domains in the affine-additive group and given a family $\Gamma$ of closed rectifiable curves in $\Omega$, we have \begin{equation}\Mod_4\left(\gamma\in\Gamma:f\circ\gamma not absolutely continuous\right)=0.\end{equation}. \bibliographystyle{plain \Addresses.}
